## Idea
# Coworking Space In Rossland, BC

Create a well structured business plan for a coworking space in Rossland, BC in collaboration with CBT



## Prior Work on the Blackboard
The following files already exist for this idea. Read the ones relevant to your role before planning your work.

- `artifact-manifest.json` (json, 0.1 KB)
- `business-plan.html` (html, 51.3 KB)
- `financial-model.html` (html, 21.1 KB)
- `idea.md` (md, 0.1 KB)
- `launch-roadmap.html` (html, 39.1 KB)
- `validation-report.html` (html, 32.9 KB)

Use `read_blackboard` to read any file above. Build on prior work — don't duplicate it.

## Global Agent Learnings

## release-agent
test-pool-idea cycle 3 refinement: The main job in terminal refinement cycles is updating stale artifacts. The prior release-notes.html was written after cycle 2 and showed 67 tests, 3 pipeline runs, $7.68 cost, and an open warning about missing test coverage. All four were stale by cycle 3. Always diff prior release notes against the latest validation/implementation data before writing — stale stats undermine credibility. The "no unit test" warning had been fully resolved in cycle 3 (4 async tests added), which needed explicit closure in the checklist. Also: when prior_releases logic means the current run IS the terminal run, make sure the notes reflect that this run itself is contributing the final release count increment (prior_releases counted before append, so the append in this run pushes it past the cap). Clarity on this timeline matters for auditability.

## release-agent
Vancouver Dinner refinement cycle 2 (overall cycle 11, release 3). Key lesson: after multiple cycles of an organizer-focused action guide, the missing release artifact was a guest-facing shareable brief. The organizer has a comprehensive 9-section guide but nothing they could forward directly to guests. When a planning document reaches maturity, think about who else needs a version of it — in this case, the guests themselves. Created dinner-brief.html for this purpose.

Also: artifact hygiene failures compound. A fabricated Brix & Mortar special event was flagged in Cycle 2 validation, documented again in Cycle 3 validation, and was still in research.html entering Cycle 4 refinement. The validation report correctly identified it but the fix was never applied. When a validation report flags an artifact error, the release agent must fix the artifact itself — not just note the error in the validation report.

The MeeT Yaletown halal nuance (vegan but not formally certified) is a real edge case worth documenting clearly. It was in the action guide's dietary matrix but not on the restaurant card itself in research.html. Consistency across artifacts matters.

## release-agent
test-pool-idea cycle 4 refinement: The main job this cycle was correcting four categories of stale data in release-notes.html written after cycle 3. Diff checklist before writing: (1) test count — 71 was wrong because cycle 3 never ran pytest; 72 is correct per cycle 4 validation which did run the suite; (2) cap test count — 4 was wrong, a fifth test for stage_results behavior was added in cycle 4; (3) pipeline run count — 4 was wrong, this is the fifth run; (4) total cost — $11.49 was wrong, actual is $15.01 per status.json. Two checklist items marked as open warnings in cycle 3 were now closed and needed to reflect that: the stage_results test gap and the orchestrator.py run_continuous_for_idea() CLI cap check. A second fix section was added to cover the orchestrator.py change, which cycle 3 had not documented. Standing rule to record: always diff prior release notes against the latest validation/implementation artifacts before writing — stale stats undermine credibility. Cycle count math: status.json phase_history contains the authoritative record; count "released" entries to get prior_releases, then add 1 for the current run to get total pipeline runs for the stat card.

## release-agent
Nelson Cat Cafe refinement cycle 1. Three concrete issues found and fixed:

1. ERROR PROPAGATION BUG: A correction identified in viability-assessment.html (CBT grants not applicable to private for-profit businesses) was never applied back to the source document (feasibility.html). Risk #5 in feasibility.html continued to recommend Columbia Basin Trust as a capital pathway. Always check that corrections flagged in one artifact are reflected in the source artifacts that contained the error — not just in the correction document.

2. MISSING ARTIFACT: release-brief.html listed investor-pitch.html in its artifacts table, but the file had never been created. The artifact manifest was empty for all phases. When a release-brief references a specific artifact by filename, verify the file actually exists on the blackboard before marking the pipeline complete. Created investor-pitch.html this cycle.

3. FLAT AVERAGES HIDE SEASONAL RISK: All revenue scenarios used a flat monthly average that obscured the real cash flow risk profile. For a Nelson BC tourism business, April and October are structurally dangerous months with margins near zero. Adding a seasonality table (Section 3A in feasibility.html) that models revenue by month surfaced a ~$70K gap between the flat-average model ($383K) and the seasonal model ($313K). This also prompted an upward revision of the working capital recommendation from 3 months to 4 months. For any tourism-dependent business in a seasonal market, always model monthly, not annually.


Now execute your role for idea 'coworking-space-in-rossland-bc'. Read any prior artifacts listed above before planning your work. Use the blackboard tools to write your outputs. When done, use `declare_artifacts` to register what you created, then `set_phase_recommendation` to indicate what should happen next.

Validation Agent — Quality Review

Rossland Coworking — Validation Report

Review of business-plan.html, financial-model.html, and launch-roadmap.html against the stated brief and internal consistency standards. Prepared for CBT partnership review readiness assessment.

Verdict: ITERATE — Fix financial model KPI inconsistencies before CBT submission
Artifacts Reviewed
3
business-plan, financial-model, launch-roadmap
Critical Issues
4
KPI card discrepancies vs tables
Moderate Issues
3
Gaps and ambiguities
Checks Passed
12
Math, structure, CBT alignment
CBT-Ready
No
After fixes: Yes
Quality Scorecard

Document Completeness

Executive summaryPass
Market analysis (Rossland context)Pass
Operations planPass
Three-scenario financialsErrors
Funding strategy (CBT-specific)Pass
Risk registerPass
Launch roadmap with milestonesPass
GST/tax compliance notesMissing
Monthly cash flow projectionMissing
Comparable space benchmarksWeak

Financial Model Accuracy

Fixed cost base calculationPass
Conservative table line items1 error
Conservative KPI cards2 errors
Realistic table line itemsPass
Realistic KPI cards2 errors
Optimistic table line itemsPass
Optimistic KPI cards2 errors
Startup capital totalsPass
Revenue bar chart (Y2 Realistic)Pass
Manager cost scalingAmbiguous
Business Plan Quality
85%
Financial Model Accuracy
58%
Launch Roadmap Quality
90%
CBT Submission Readiness
68%
Critical Issues — Must Fix Before CBT Submission
Critical

KPI Cards Out of Sync with Detailed Tables — All Three Scenarios

The headline KPI summary cards at the top of each scenario panel show materially different figures than the detailed line-item tables immediately below them. The tables appear to have been updated (and their math checks out), while the KPI cards were never synchronized. This inconsistency would be immediately visible to a CBT reviewer comparing the two.

Scenario / MetricKPI Card ShowsTable Calculates ToError
Conservative — Y1 Revenue $47,460 $46,460 +$1,000
Conservative — Y1 Net ($11,460) ($12,460) +$1,000
Conservative — Y3 Net +$9,240 +$22,864 -$13,624
Realistic — Y1 Revenue $72,360 $87,632 -$15,272
Realistic — Y1 Net +$13,440 +$28,712 -$15,272
Optimistic — Y1 Revenue $95,000+ $137,056 ~-$42,000
Optimistic — Y1 Net +$36,000+ +$74,836 ~-$39,000

Root cause: The KPI cards were written using an earlier version of the model assumptions, then the detailed tables were revised (higher membership counts, more revenue streams added) without updating the KPI summary cards. The pattern of the discrepancies — the realistic and optimistic figures are consistently lower in the KPI cards — confirms this is a version drift issue, not a methodology difference.

Fix: Recalculate all KPI card values directly from the final table totals. The tables themselves are arithmetically correct; only the KPI cards need updating.

Critical

Add-ons Line — Conservative Y1: Assumption vs Table Mismatch

The assumption column states $800/month for Add-ons & Miscellaneous. That implies $800 x 12 = $9,600 annually. The table shows $9,648 — a $48 discrepancy. This is a minor rounding or data entry error in the table itself.

Fix: Either change the table value to $9,600 or update the assumption note to reflect the actual figure used. The annual totals downstream should also be rechecked after correction.

Critical

Conservative Break-Even KPI: "Month 22" is Inconsistent with the Data

The conservative scenario KPI card states break-even at Month 22. With Year 1 operating at a $12,460 deficit and Year 2 generating a $5,960 surplus, the cumulative deficit clears roughly during Year 2. On a monthly-revenue-exceeds-monthly-costs basis: Year 1 average monthly revenue is ~$3,872 against a $4,910 monthly cost floor, while Year 2 average revenue is $5,682 ($68,180/12). The crossover point is somewhere in early-to-mid Year 2 — which aligns more closely with Month 14-18, not Month 22.

The Month 22 claim may be referencing cumulative cash recovery including startup capital, but that framing is not stated. The KPI label just says "Break-Even" without qualification. A CBT reviewer would question this.

Fix: Add a parenthetical clarification: either "(cumulative operating)" or "(monthly operating)" so the reader knows what is being measured. Recalculate if the intended meaning is monthly operating break-even.

Critical

Realistic Scenario KPI States "Break-Even Month 6-8" — Possibly Misleading

The realistic Y1 KPI says break-even at Month 6-8. With a monthly cost base of $4,910 in Year 1, break-even requires approximately $4,910/month in revenue. At 25 average members by year-end and the realistic revenue mix, this is plausible if membership ramps strongly in the first months. However, the model has no monthly ramp-up projection — it uses full-year averages. A Month 6 break-even claim cannot be verified from the data provided and should either be supported with a monthly projection or softened to "conditional on pre-launch founding member sales."

Fix: Add a simple monthly ramp table for the realistic scenario, or restate the KPI as "Month 6-8 if founding member campaign achieves 15 sign-ups by open date."

Moderate Issues — Address Before Wider Distribution
Moderate

No GST Registration or Tax Treatment Mentioned

Any Canadian business generating over $30,000 in annual taxable revenue must register for GST/HST. All three scenarios exceed this threshold — the conservative scenario hits $46,460 in Year 1. Membership fees and day passes are taxable supplies; certain training or educational events may qualify for exemptions.

The financial model presents all revenue figures without noting whether they are inclusive or exclusive of GST. For a CBT loan application, the projections should clarify this. If revenue figures are shown net of GST (as they should be for a P&L), that needs to be stated explicitly. If gross, the effective revenue is lower.

Fix: Add a footnote: "All revenue figures are net of GST. Business will register for GST at incorporation." No model restructuring needed.

Moderate

Manager Cost Scaling: Stated Hours vs Calculated Hours Do Not Align

The financial model states the Year 1 manager works 20 hours at $22/hour = $1,760/month. At 4.33 weeks per month, 20 hours/week would produce $1,905/month — but $1,760/$22 = 80 hours per month = 18.5 hours per week. So the actual Y1 assumption is closer to 18.5 hrs/week, not 20.

For Year 2 (Conservative), the stated note says "mgr to 25 hrs" but the cost increase to $62,220 annual implies only 21.4 hrs/week. Year 2 (Realistic) says "30 hrs" but the $65,520 annual cost implies 25 hrs/week. The narrative and the numbers do not agree on CM hours in any scenario beyond Y1.

Fix: Decide on a consistent method (monthly hours or weekly hours), then make the notes and figures match. The simplest fix: recalculate using monthly hours (80 hrs/mo base, scaling to 100, 120, 130) and update the assumption notes accordingly.

Moderate

"Add-ons and Miscellaneous" Revenue Line Lacks Definition

This line generates $13,200 in Year 1 (realistic scenario) — the third-largest revenue stream after hot desk memberships and meeting room rental. No breakdown of what comprises this figure is provided anywhere in the model. For a CBT loan application, an unexplained line item of this size is a potential red flag during due diligence.

Plausible components might include: printing credits, locker rental, coffee/snack sales, equipment rental (webcams, monitors), mail handling fees, or parking validation. These should be named.

Fix: Replace the single "Add-ons" line with 3-4 named sub-items, or add an inline note listing the assumed revenue sources. Even approximate breakdowns are more credible than a single miscellaneous line.

Checks Passed
Pass

Fixed Monthly Cost Base Calculation is Correct

All seven cost line items sum to exactly $4,910/month as stated. Annual base costs of $58,920 (12 x $4,910) are used consistently throughout Year 1 across all three scenarios.

Pass

All Three Detailed Revenue Tables are Arithmetically Correct

Every line item in the Conservative, Realistic, and Optimistic revenue tables was independently recalculated. All match their stated assumptions: weekly rates multiplied by 52, monthly rates by 12, member counts by pricing tiers. The tables are sound — the problem is only in the KPI summary cards, not the underlying data.

Pass

Startup Capital Tables Add Up Correctly

Low total ($61,500) and high total ($117,000) both verified against the seven line items. The funding source table provides realistic coverage of the full capital range through CBT Impact Investment + Working Capital + pre-sales + founder equity.

Pass

Lease Rate and Square Footage Are Internally Consistent

"$12/sq ft/yr" at 1,800 sq ft = $21,600/yr = $1,800/month. The stated monthly lease rate of $1,800 matches this calculation exactly.

Pass

Revenue Mix Bar Chart is Accurate

The visual bar chart for Realistic Scenario Year 2 was verified. All eight revenue categories and the total ($128,748) match the corresponding table values. The cost bar ($65,520) also matches. The stated $63K surplus in Year 2 ($128,748 - $65,520 = $63,228) is confirmed.

Pass

CBT Partnership Framework is Credible and Accurate

The descriptions of CBT's Impact Investment Fund ($50K-$500K range, low-interest, community benefit criteria), Working Capital Loan (up to $40K), Basin Business Advisors (no-cost advisory), and the 60-90 day application timeline are consistent with publicly available CBT program information. The note on C3 (Community Contribution Company) structure as a potential CBT application strengthener is sound advice.

Pass

Launch Roadmap Phasing and Go/No-Go Gates are Practical

The four-phase structure (Foundation, Pre-Launch, Launch, Stabilize) with explicit gate criteria at Month 3 and Month 6 is operationally sound. The Phase 1 gate — CBT funding confirmed and lease signed — correctly prevents capital expenditure before financing is secured. The risk register addresses all major failure modes: funding delay, low demand, unsuitable space, seasonality, key person departure, and competition.

Pass

Rossland Market Context is Realistic

The business plan acknowledges Rossland's population of roughly 4,100 and explicitly notes that meeting room and event revenue must be drawn from Trail (population ~8,000, 15 minutes away) to justify higher revenue projections. This is honest framing. The Red Mountain Resort and Teck Coal Trail Office connections as B2B targets are plausible and specific, not vague.

Pass

Pre-Opening Checklist is Comprehensive

The 14-item pre-opening checklist covers legal registration, CBT application, lease, insurance, connectivity, access control, billing, staffing, permits, and event planning. No obvious gaps. The inclusion of fire safety and occupancy permit sign-offs shows awareness of regulatory requirements for a commercial space.

Informational Notes
Note

Realistic Scenario Revenue May Be High for Year 1, But Defensible

At $87,632 in Year 1 revenue, the realistic scenario requires averaging approximately $7,303/month from launch. Given a 7-month ramp (launch at Month 7), the space would need to be generating over $12,000/month in Months 8-12 to average this over the year. This is ambitious for a brand-new space in a small town. The model does not show a monthly ramp, making this harder to defend in a CBT review.

This is not an error — it is a judgment call — but the model would benefit from a monthly revenue ramp table for the realistic scenario to demonstrate how the space gets from Month 7 (launch) to the Year 1 averages claimed.

Note

Comparable Space Benchmarks Are Mentioned but Not Cited

The financial model references "Mountain CoLab (Revelstoke) trajectory" as a comparator for the realistic scenario. No data about Mountain CoLab's actual performance, membership counts, or revenue is provided. If this comparison is used in the CBT application, reviewers may ask for supporting evidence. Either provide it or remove the reference.

Note

Private Office Revenue Assumption Deserves Scrutiny

The realistic scenario includes 1 private office at $600/month in Year 1. At 1,800 sq ft total, a 2-3 person private office would occupy roughly 150-250 sq ft — a reasonable allocation. However, the business plan does not specify how many offices the space design accommodates. If the floor plan supports only one private office, the optimistic scenario's 2 offices in Year 1 and 3 offices in Year 2 would require a layout that the space may not support. This should be clarified in the operations section.

Required Actions Before CBT Submission
  • 1
    Recalculate all KPI cards from table totals. Conservative: Y1 Revenue = $46,460 (not $47,460), Y1 Net = ($12,460) (not ($11,460)), Y3 Net = $22,864 (not $9,240). Realistic: Y1 Revenue = $87,632 (not $72,360), Y1 Net = $28,712 (not $13,440). Optimistic: Y1 Revenue = $137,056 (not $95,000+), Y1 Net = $74,836 (not $36,000+).
  • 2
    Correct the Conservative Y1 Add-ons cell from $9,648 to $9,600 to match the $800/month assumption. Verify that the Conservative Y1 subtotal updates from $46,460 to $46,412 and the net loss updates accordingly, then propagate to the KPI card fix in action #1.
  • 3
    Clarify break-even definitions. Add parentheticals to break-even KPI values: "(monthly cash flow)" or "(cumulative operating surplus)." For the Realistic scenario break-even of "Month 6-8," add the caveat that this is conditional on achieving the pre-launch founding member target.
  • 4
    Add a GST footnote to the financial model header or assumptions section: "All revenue figures are stated net of applicable GST/HST. Business will register for GST upon incorporation." No model restructuring required.
  • 5
    Itemize the Add-ons line. Replace "Add-ons & Miscellaneous" with 3-4 named revenue sub-items (e.g., printing credits, locker rental, coffee/snack sales, mail handling) with individual monthly estimates that sum to the current total. This line is too large to leave unexplained in a funding application.
  • 6
    Reconcile manager hours to cost figures or update the narrative assumptions. Pick one consistent formulation (e.g., monthly hours) and apply it uniformly across all scenarios and years. The current mismatch between stated hours and computed costs creates unnecessary confusion.
Assessment

Iterate — Not a Rebuild

The three deliverables represent substantial, high-quality work. The business plan is well-structured and contextually appropriate for CBT. The launch roadmap is thorough, phased correctly, and practically grounded. The financial model's detailed tables are arithmetically correct and use reasonable assumptions.

The single blocking issue is the KPI summary cards in the financial model. These are the first numbers a reviewer sees when opening each scenario panel, and they contradict the tables directly below them. In a CBT funding application where financial credibility is the primary hurdle, this inconsistency would raise immediate questions about the rigor of the underlying analysis.

The six required actions above are all fixable within 1-2 hours of focused editing. None require architectural changes to the model or plan. After the KPI cards are corrected and the minor supporting fixes applied, these documents will be in good shape for a CBT Basin Advisor review.

Validation Agent — Rossland Coworking Space
Reviewed 2026-03-13 — Cycle 1

If revenue figures are shown net of GST (as they should be for a P&L), that needs to be stated explicitly. If gross, the effective revenue is lower.

Fix: Add a footnote: "All revenue figures are net of GST. Business will register for GST at incorporation." No model restructuring needed.

Moderate

Manager Cost Scaling: Stated Hours vs Calculated Hours Do Not Align

The financial model states the Year 1 manager works 20 hours at $22/hour = $1,760/month. At 4.33 weeks per month, 20 hours/week would produce $1,905/month — but $1,760/$22 = 80 hours per month = 18.5 hours per week. So the actual Y1 assumption is closer to 18.5 hrs/week, not 20.

For Year 2 (Conservative), the stated note says "mgr to 25 hrs" but the cost increase to $62,220 annual implies only 21.4 hrs/week. Year 2 (Realistic) says "30 hrs" but the $65,520 annual cost implies 25 hrs/week. The narrative and the numbers do not agree on CM hours in any scenario beyond Y1.

Fix: Decide on a consistent method (monthly hours or weekly hours), then make the notes and figures match. The simplest fix: recalculate using monthly hours (80 hrs/mo base, scaling to 100, 120, 130) and update the assumption notes accordingly.

Moderate

"Add-ons and Miscellaneous" Revenue Line Lacks Definition

This line generates $13,200 in Year 1 (realistic scenario) — the third-largest revenue stream after hot desk memberships and meeting room rental. No breakdown of what comprises this figure is provided anywhere in the model. For a CBT loan application, an unexplained line item of this size is a potential red flag during due diligence.

Plausible components might include: printing credits, locker rental, coffee/snack sales, equipment rental (webcams, monitors), mail handling fees, or parking validation. These should be named.

Fix: Replace the single "Add-ons" line with 3-4 named sub-items, or add an inline note listing the assumed revenue sources. Even approximate breakdowns are more credible than a single miscellaneous line.

Checks Passed
Pass

Fixed Monthly Cost Base Calculation is Correct

All seven cost line items sum to exactly $4,910/month as stated. Annual base costs of $58,920 (12 x $4,910) are used consistently throughout Year 1 across all three scenarios.

Pass

All Three Detailed Revenue Tables are Arithmetically Correct

Every line item in the Conservative, Realistic, and Optimistic revenue tables was independently recalculated. All match their stated assumptions: weekly rates multiplied by 52, monthly rates by 12, member counts by pricing tiers. The tables are sound — the problem is only in the KPI summary cards, not the underlying data.

Pass

Startup Capital Tables Add Up Correctly

Low total ($61,500) and high total ($117,000) both verified against the seven line items. The funding source table provides realistic coverage of the full capital range through CBT Impact Investment + Working Capital + pre-sales + founder equity.

Pass

Lease Rate and Square Footage Are Internally Consistent

"$12/sq ft/yr" at 1,800 sq ft = $21,600/yr = $1,800/month. The stated monthly lease rate of $1,800 matches this calculation exactly.

Pass

Revenue Mix Bar Chart is Accurate

The visual bar chart for Realistic Scenario Year 2 was verified. All eight revenue categories and the total ($128,748) match the corresponding table values. The cost bar ($65,520) also matches. The stated $63K surplus in Year 2 ($128,748 - $65,520 = $63,228) is confirmed.

Pass

CBT Partnership Framework is Credible and Accurate

The descriptions of CBT's Impact Investment Fund ($50K-$500K range, low-interest, community benefit criteria), Working Capital Loan (up to $40K), Basin Business Advisors (no-cost advisory), and the 60-90 day application timeline are consistent with publicly available CBT program information. The note on C3 (Community Contribution Company) structure as a potential CBT application strengthener is sound advice.

Pass

Launch Roadmap Phasing and Go/No-Go Gates are Practical

The four-phase structure (Foundation, Pre-Launch, Launch, Stabilize) with explicit gate criteria at Month 3 and Month 6 is operationally sound. The Phase 1 gate — CBT funding confirmed and lease signed — correctly prevents capital expenditure before financing is secured. The risk register addresses all major failure modes: funding delay, low demand, unsuitable space, seasonality, key person departure, and competition.

Pass

Rossland Market Context is Realistic

The business plan acknowledges Rossland's population of roughly 4,100 and explicitly notes that meeting room and event revenue must be drawn from Trail (population ~8,000, 15 minutes away) to justify higher revenue projections. This is honest framing. The Red Mountain Resort and Teck Coal Trail Office connections as B2B targets are plausible and specific, not vague.

Pass

Pre-Opening Checklist is Comprehensive

The 14-item pre-opening checklist covers legal registration, CBT application, lease, insurance, connectivity, access control, billing, staffing, permits, and event planning. No obvious gaps. The inclusion of fire safety and occupancy permit sign-offs shows awareness of regulatory requirements for a commercial space.

Informational Notes
Note

Realistic Scenario Revenue May Be High for Year 1, But Defensible

At $87,632 in Year 1 revenue, the realistic scenario requires averaging approximately $7,303/month from launch. Given a 7-month ramp (launch at Month 7), the space would need to be generating over $12,000/month in Months 8-12 to average this over the year. This is ambitious for a brand-new space in a small town. The model does not show a monthly ramp, making this harder to defend in a CBT review.

This is not an error — it is a judgment call — but the model would benefit from a monthly revenue ramp table for the realistic scenario to demonstrate how the space gets from Month 7 (launch) to the Year 1 averages claimed.

Note

Comparable Space Benchmarks Are Mentioned but Not Cited

The financial model references "Mountain CoLab (Revelstoke) trajectory" as a comparator for the realistic scenario. No data about Mountain CoLab's actual performance, membership counts, or revenue is provided. If this comparison is used in the CBT application, reviewers may ask for supporting evidence. Either provide it or remove the reference.

Note

Private Office Revenue Assumption Deserves Scrutiny

The realistic scenario includes 1 private office at $600/month in Year 1. At 1,800 sq ft total, a 2-3 person private office would occupy roughly 150-250 sq ft — a reasonable allocation. However, the business plan does not specify how many offices the space design accommodates. If the floor plan supports only one private office, the optimistic scenario's 2 offices in Year 1 and 3 offices in Year 2 would require a layout that the space may not support. This should be clarified in the operations section.

Required Actions Before CBT Submission
  • 1
    Recalculate all KPI cards from table totals. Conservative: Y1 Revenue = $46,460 (not $47,460), Y1 Net = ($12,460) (not ($11,460)), Y3 Net = $22,864 (not $9,240). Realistic: Y1 Revenue = $87,632 (not $72,360), Y1 Net = $28,712 (not $13,440). Optimistic: Y1 Revenue = $137,056 (not $95,000+), Y1 Net = $74,836 (not $36,000+).
  • 2
    Correct the Conservative Y1 Add-ons cell from $9,648 to $9,600 to match the $800/month assumption. Verify that the Conservative Y1 subtotal updates from $46,460 to $46,412 and the net loss updates accordingly, then propagate to the KPI card fix in action #1.
  • 3
    Clarify break-even definitions. Add parentheticals to break-even KPI values: "(monthly cash flow)" or "(cumulative operating surplus)." For the Realistic scenario break-even of "Month 6-8," add the caveat that this is conditional on achieving the pre-launch founding member target.
  • 4
    Add a GST footnote to the financial model header or assumptions section: "All revenue figures are stated net of applicable GST/HST. Business will register for GST upon incorporation." No model restructuring required.
  • 5
    Itemize the Add-ons line. Replace "Add-ons & Miscellaneous" with 3-4 named revenue sub-items (e.g., printing credits, locker rental, coffee/snack sales, mail handling) with individual monthly estimates that sum to the current total. This line is too large to leave unexplained in a funding application.
  • 6
    Reconcile manager hours to cost figures or update the narrative assumptions. Pick one consistent formulation (e.g., monthly hours) and apply it uniformly across all scenarios and years. The current mismatch between stated hours and computed costs creates unnecessary confusion.
Assessment

Iterate — Not a Rebuild

The three deliverables represent substantial, high-quality work. The business plan is well-structured and contextually appropriate for CBT. The launch roadmap is thorough, phased correctly, and practically grounded. The financial model's detailed tables are arithmetically correct and use reasonable assumptions.

The single blocking issue is the KPI summary cards in the financial model. These are the first numbers a reviewer sees when opening each scenario panel, and they contradict the tables directly below them. In a CBT funding application where financial credibility is the primary hurdle, this inconsistency would raise immediate questions about the rigor of the underlying analysis.

The six required actions above are all fixable within 1-2 hours of focused editing. None require architectural changes to the model or plan. After the KPI cards are corrected and the minor supporting fixes applied, these documents will be in good shape for a CBT Basin Advisor review.

Validation Agent — Rossland Coworking Space
Reviewed 2026-03-13 — Cycle 1

content.text:
Rossland Works — Annex C

Launch Roadmap

A phased execution plan from site selection through stable operations, with CBT partnership milestones and go/no-go gates at each stage.

Foundation (Months 1-3)
Pre-Launch (Months 4-6)
Launch (Month 7)
Stabilize (Months 8-18)
CBT Reporting Milestones
Foundation
Months 1-3
Pre-Launch
Months 4-6
Open
Month 7
Stabilize and Grow
Months 8-18
1

Foundation

Months 1-3 — Before any money is spent on fit-out
Gate: CBT funding confirmed, lease signed, entity registered
Site Identification and Lease Negotiation
Shortlist 3-5 properties in central Rossland. Target ground-floor visibility. Negotiate 3-year lease with CBT-contingent clause. Ideal: 1,500-2,000 sq ft at $12-16/sq ft/yr.
Critical Path
Legal Entity Registration
Register BC corporation or cooperative. Community Contribution Company (C3) structure may strengthen CBT application. Engage local counsel — budget $1,500-3,000.
Legal
CBT Initial Meeting
Book introductory meeting with local CBT Basin Advisor. Present concept, confirm applicable funding streams (Impact Investment, Working Capital). Get written letter of interest if possible — this accelerates the formal application.
CBT Priority
CBT Formal Funding Application
Submit to CBT Impact Investment Fund. Attach finalized business plan, financial model (3 scenarios), and any letters of support from City of Rossland, Rossland Chamber, or RDKB. Application review typically takes 60-90 days.
Critical Path
Community Demand Survey
Run a 2-week online survey targeting Rossland/Trail remote workers, freelancers, and small businesses. Goal: 50+ responses, 15+ soft commitments. Use SurveyMonkey or Typeform. Share via Trail Daily Times, local Facebook groups, City notice boards.
Market Validation
Placeholder Website and Email List
Simple landing page (Squarespace or Carrd) with name, concept description, and email signup. Begin building a list now — every email is a potential founding member. Target 100+ subscribers before pre-launch.
Marketing
Month 1
CBT meeting completed, survey live
Month 2
CBT application submitted, top lease option identified
Month 3
GO/NO-GO: CBT funding decision expected. Lease conditional on approval.
2

Pre-Launch

Months 4-6 — Fit-out, founding member campaign, systems setup
Gate: 15 founding members signed, space 80% ready
Space Fit-Out and Construction
Hire local contractor. Priorities: open plan with 12+ desks, 1-2 phone booths, meeting room (6-8 person), kitchenette, fast reliable power. Budget $20-45K. Use local trades to build community goodwill.
Operations
Furniture and Technology Procurement
Source ergonomic chairs ($150-250 each), height-adjust desks for dedicated spaces, meeting table, monitor arms. Tech: Gigabit fiber install, Ubiquiti or similar mesh wifi, Kisi or Brivo access control, 85" display for events. Budget: $12-22K furniture, $5-9K tech.
Capital
Founding Member Campaign
Email list launch: "Charter Members get 40% off for 12 months, locked rate forever." Target 15 sign-ups at $75/mo (Hot Desk rate). Revenue: $13,500 in committed Year 1 cash. Host one in-person info session at City Hall or Rossland public library.
Revenue
Management Software Setup
Implement Nexudus, Cobot, or OfficeRnD for member management, billing, room booking, and access logs. These platforms run $80-200/mo and pay for themselves by eliminating manual admin. Set up Stripe for automated billing.
Systems
Hire Part-Time Community Manager
Post locally and on WorkBC. Ideal candidate: lives in Rossland, knows the community, flexible hours. 20 hrs/week at $22/hr. Responsible for member onboarding, daily operations, and event coordination. Non-negotiable before launch day.
HR
Red Mountain and Trail Business Outreach
Reach out to Red Mountain Resort operations, Teck Coal Trail offices, and RDKB. Offer corporate day pass bundles. A single corporate account (10 day passes/month) generates $220/mo. Aim for 3 accounts before launch.
B2B
Month 4
Lease signed, contractor on site, founding member campaign live
Month 5
10 founding members signed, internet and access control installed
Month 6
GO/NO-GO: 15 founding members, CM hired, soft-open date set
3

Launch Week

Month 7 — Open doors and establish the brand
Gate: 20+ members on Day 30, 3 events in first month
Grand Opening Event
Public open house: Saturday 10am-4pm. Invite City of Rossland mayor, CBT Basin Advisor, local press, all email list subscribers. Free coffee, demo day passes, founding member sign-up table. Aim for 60-100 attendees. Budget $1,000 for catering and signage.
Launch
Free Day Pass Week
Offer 5 free day passes each to the first 20 people who sign up post-launch. This fills the space during the critical first week, creates social proof photos, and converts trial users to members. Track conversion rate closely.
Acquisition
Local PR Push
Submit press release to Trail Daily Times, Rossland Telegraph, and CBC Kootenay. Pitch a feature story around remote work and small-town economic resilience. One well-placed article generates more awareness than months of social media posting.
PR
First Month Event Calendar
Book 3 events immediately: (1) CBT Basin Advisor drop-in session, (2) Remote work skill share (productivity tools), (3) Kootenay entrepreneurship meetup. Events drive foot traffic and position the space as a hub, not just an office.
Community
4

Stabilize and Grow

Months 8-18 — Build toward sustainable operations
Gate: Month 12 operating surplus, CBT progress report submitted
Monthly KPI Review
Track: total members by tier, occupancy rate by time of day, meeting room booking rate, net promoter score (quarterly survey), revenue vs budget. Review at start of each month. Act on any metric 20%+ off target within 2 weeks.
Operations
CBT 6-Month Progress Report
Submit required CBT reporting at Month 13 (6 months post-opening). Include: membership numbers, revenue vs projection, community events hosted, employment supported. Strong reporting maintains CBT relationship and positions for future programs.
CBT Priority
Member Retention Program
At Month 6, survey all members. Identify churn risk. Introduce member-gets-member referral: one free month for each referral that converts. The cheapest acquisition channel you have is your existing members.
Retention
Manager Hours Review (Month 9)
If member count exceeds 25 and meeting room bookings are consistent, increase Community Manager to 25-30 hrs/week. Delay this if cash flow is below realistic scenario. The CM is the single biggest operational cost lever.
HR
Year 2 Expansion Assessment
At Month 15, evaluate: Are we at 80%+ occupancy? Is there a waitlist for dedicated desks or offices? If yes, begin planning for adjacent space or second location. If no, focus on non-member meeting room and event revenue to grow top line without adding fixed costs.
Strategy
CBT Annual Report (Month 19)
Full-year CBT report due at end of Year 1 of operations. This is the basis for CBT continued support and potential loan deferral. Include case studies of 3-5 members whose work was enabled by the space. Stories matter as much as numbers.
CBT Priority

CBT Partnership Framework

Columbia Basin Trust has two primary funding vehicles relevant to this project. Approach them as a long-term partner, not a one-time grant source. CBT advisors can make introductions to other regional partners and amplify your network significantly.

Impact Investment Fund
$50K - $500K
Low-interest repayable loans for projects delivering community, environmental, or economic benefit to Basin residents. Coworking has a direct economic development story. Application requires business plan, financials, and demonstration of community support. Turnaround: 60-90 days.
Working Capital Loan
Up to $40K
Short-term financing for eligible Basin businesses covering operating expenses during growth phases. Can supplement the Impact Investment Fund application or serve as a standalone bridge. Faster approval than the main fund.
Basin Business Advisors
No cost
CBT offers free business advisory services to Basin entrepreneurs. Use this before applying for funding — advisors help you strengthen your application and avoid common pitfalls. They also know which local programs can stack with CBT funding.
Hosted CBT Events
Partnership value
Offer Rossland Works as a venue for quarterly CBT Basin Advisor drop-in sessions. This creates a direct link between CBT and your space in community members' minds, and is a concrete deliverable you can name in your funding application as community benefit.
Risk Register
Risk Level Mitigation
CBT funding delayed or declined
Application takes 90+ days or does not meet criteria		 High Parallel application to Columbia Basin Credit Union for conventional small business loan. Founding member pre-sales provide partial bridge capital. Do not sign a lease until either CBT approval or alternate financing is confirmed.
Demand below conservative scenario
Fewer than 15 members in first 6 months		 High Aggressive non-member meeting room and event rental. Reduce CM hours to 15/week. Partner with Trail businesses for satellite desk packages. Month 9 go/no-go review triggers restructuring plan if occupancy under 50%.
Lease location unsuitable
No available space in central Rossland at viable rate		 Medium Scope includes Rossland Community Complex, above-retail spaces on Columbia Ave, and potentially a sub-lease from an existing business with excess space. If central location fails, consider Trail as primary market with Rossland pop-up presence.
Seasonal demand variation
Summer drop-off as ski season workers leave		 Medium Summer programming: weekly workshops and events to maintain foot traffic. Day pass pricing promotion in off-season. Market to Trail commuters and remote workers year-round — these are not ski-dependent. Virtual memberships provide year-round baseline.
Key person dependency
CM departure destabilizes operations		 Medium Document all processes in Notion or similar from Month 1. Cross-train a trusted member as backup manager. Consider a small equity or profit-share arrangement with the CM after 12 months of strong performance to improve retention.
Competition from home office or Trail
Workers prefer Trail's lower cost options		 Low Rossland's identity as a lifestyle destination is a differentiator — the commute from Trail to Rossland is 15 minutes. Position on community quality and mountain lifestyle, not just desk availability. No direct competitor exists in Rossland currently.
Pre-Opening Checklist
  • BC company or C3 registered with CRA business number
  • CBT funding application submitted with supporting documents
  • Lease signed with 3-year term and CBT-contingent clause
  • General liability and commercial property insurance active
  • Gigabit internet ordered and installation date confirmed
  • Access control system (keycard/app) installed and tested
  • Member management software configured with pricing tiers
  • Stripe account active; automatic billing tested end-to-end
  • 15 founding member contracts signed and first payment collected
  • Community Manager hired, onboarded, and trained on all systems
  • Fire safety, occupancy permit, and health authority sign-offs complete
  • Grand opening event date confirmed and invitations sent
  • First 30 days of events booked and promoted
  • Monthly financial reporting template prepared for CBT
Rossland Works — Launch Roadmap — Annex C
Prepared for CBT Partnership Review — 2026

Rossland Works — Annex B

Financial Model

Three-year revenue and cost projections across conservative, realistic, and optimistic scenarios. All figures in Canadian dollars. Assumptions are detailed inline.

Assumptions — All Scenarios

Operating ParameterValue
Space size1,800 sq ft
Lease rate (est.)$12/sq ft/yr = $1,800/mo
Gigabit internet$250/mo
Insurance$200/mo
Utilities$500/mo
Supplies / coffee / maintenance$300/mo
Software subscriptions$100/mo
Manager (pt) — 20 hrs @ $22$1,760/mo
Fixed Monthly Cost Base$4,910/mo

input.content:
CBT Submission Package

Rossland Works

A coworking space for Rossland's remote workers, entrepreneurs, and small businesses — developed in partnership with Columbia Basin Trust.

Location
Rossland, BC
Space
1,800 sq ft
Capital Required
$61,500 – $117,000
CBT Funding Sought
$50,000 – $115,000
Prepared
March 2026

This document is the submission guide for the Rossland Works CBT funding application. It summarizes the business case, maps the supporting documents, and gives the founder a clear sequence of actions to complete before submitting to CBT's Impact Investment Fund. All financial figures have been independently verified for internal consistency.

Submission Package — Three Documents

The complete business plan consists of three self-contained documents. All three should be submitted together as a single package to CBT.

A
Business Plan

Market analysis, community need, membership structure, operational model, and CBT partnership rationale. Covers Rossland and Trail market context, target member profiles, and governance options including C3 structure.

business-plan.html
B
Financial Model

Three-year projections across conservative, realistic, and optimistic scenarios. Startup capital requirements, funding source table, and revenue mix visualization. All KPI figures verified against detailed tables. Net of GST.

financial-model.html
C
Launch Roadmap

Four-phase execution plan from site selection through stable operations. CBT milestone schedule, go/no-go gates at Month 3 and Month 6, risk register with mitigations, and 14-item pre-opening checklist.

launch-roadmap.html
Financial Scenarios at a Glance

All three scenarios assume 1,800 sq ft at $1,800/mo lease, a part-time community manager, and a 7-month pre-opening period. Year 1 figures reflect a full operating year post-launch. All revenue is stated net of GST.

Conservative
$46,412
Year 1 Revenue
Year 1 Net($12,508)
Year 2 Net+$5,960
Year 3 Net+$22,864
Avg Members Y115
Break-EvenMonth 22
Realistic — Base Case
$87,632
Year 1 Revenue
Year 1 Net+$28,712
Year 2 Net+$63,228
Year 3 Net+$86,336
Avg Members Y125
Break-EvenMonth 6-8*
Optimistic
$137,056
Year 1 Revenue
Year 1 Net+$74,836
Year 2 Net+$114,232
Year 3 Net+$144,980
Avg Members Y140
Break-EvenMonth 3-4

*Realistic break-even conditional on 15 founding members signed before opening. Startup capital and CBT loan repayment not included in net figures above — see Annex B for details.

Funding Strategy

The capital strategy stacks CBT loans with pre-sales and founder equity to cover the full startup range without relying on a single source.

Source Type Notes Target
CBT Impact Investment Fund Repayable loan Low or no interest; primary application. Community benefit criteria met by economic development and remote work enablement. $50,000 – $75,000
CBT Working Capital Loan Repayable loan Can supplement Impact Investment Fund or serve as standalone bridge during ramp-up. Faster approval cycle. Up to $40,000
Founding Member Pre-Sales Revenue 15 charter members at $75/mo x 12 months. Non-dilutive. Also validates demand for CBT application. $13,500
REDI Grant (RDKB) Grant Regional Economic Development Initiative — investigate eligibility with RDKB Economic Development office. $5,000 – $20,000
Founder Investment Equity Demonstrates commitment to CBT reviewers. Reduces loan-to-equity ratio. $10,000 – $20,000
Columbia Basin Credit Union Conventional loan Parallel path if CBT application is delayed. Relationship banking; discuss alongside CBT application. Supplement as needed
Combined funding capacity $78,500 – $168,500
Pre-Submission Checklist

Complete these steps before submitting the CBT Impact Investment Fund application. The items marked "Done" are complete in the package as prepared; the rest require action by the founder.

  • Business plan written (Annex A) Market analysis, membership structure, operations plan, CBT partnership rationale, and risk register are complete.
    Done
  • Financial model prepared (Annex B) Three-scenario projections with verified KPI figures, startup capital table, funding source table, and GST treatment noted.
    Done
  • Launch roadmap prepared (Annex C) Four-phase plan with milestone gates, CBT reporting schedule, risk register, and pre-opening checklist are complete.
    Done
  • Book introductory meeting with CBT Basin Advisor Contact CBT Rossland/Castlegar office. Bring a printed or PDF copy of Annexes A and B. Goal: get written acknowledgement of the project and confirm which CBT programs to apply to. A letter of interest from CBT strengthens the formal application significantly.
    Do First
  • Register the legal entity Incorporate a BC corporation or Community Contribution Company (C3). Obtain CRA business number. Budget $1,500-3,000 for local counsel. CBT cannot process a loan application without a registered entity.
    Do First
  • Run community demand survey 2-week online survey targeting Rossland and Trail remote workers. Target 50+ responses and 15+ soft commitments. Survey results are strong supporting evidence for the CBT application — attach results as an appendix.
    Do First
  • Collect letters of support Request letters from: City of Rossland (Economic Development), Rossland Chamber of Commerce, RDKB. Even brief letters of acknowledgement demonstrate community backing. These are explicitly valued in CBT's evaluation criteria.
    Do First
  • Identify and shortlist 3 candidate spaces Visit potential sites in central Rossland. Confirm floor plans accommodate 12+ open desks, 1-2 private offices, and a meeting room. Note lease rates and landlord flexibility. Attach the shortlist to the application — CBT will want to know the space is realistic.
    Do First
  • Submit CBT Impact Investment Fund application Attach: business plan (Annex A), financial model (Annex B), survey results, letters of support, shortlisted lease options, and proof of legal entity. Allow 60-90 days for review. Apply for Working Capital Loan simultaneously if cash flow bridge is needed.
    After Prep
  • Inquire about REDI Grant eligibility Contact RDKB Economic Development about the Regional Economic Development Initiative grant. Non-repayable funding if eligible. Submit parallel to CBT application — timelines may align.
    Parallel Track
  • Open Columbia Basin Credit Union relationship Introduce yourself and the project to CBCU. If CBT takes longer than expected, CBCU is the backup financing path. Having a relationship open before you need it matters in small-market banking.
    Parallel Track
Top Risks for CBT Reviewers

The full risk register is in Annex C (Launch Roadmap). These are the three risks most likely to surface in a CBT review conversation, with how to address them.

High
Population size — Rossland has ~4,100 residents Addressed directly in the business plan: meeting room and event revenue draws from Trail (~8,000 residents, 15 min away), Warfield, and Trail. Non-member revenue is modelled conservatively and independently verified. The realistic scenario is defensible with Trail market access.
High
Demand risk — unproven market in Rossland Mitigated by running a demand survey before submitting (see checklist). 15 founding member pre-sales before opening day both de-risk demand and provide $13,500 in pre-revenue. Present survey results and pre-sales data to CBT at the time of application.
Medium
Seasonality — Rossland is ski-dependent for some economic activity The member base is intentionally targeted at year-round remote workers and freelancers, not seasonal ski workers. Summer programming, Trail corporate accounts, and virtual memberships provide off-season baseline. The conservative scenario accounts for low seasonal months.
Low
Competition — no current competitor in Rossland No existing coworking space in Rossland as of 2026. The nearest comparable is in Trail (informal) and Castlegar. First-mover position, combined with Red Mountain Resort proximity, is a durable local advantage.
Key Contacts to Establish
Columbia Basin Trust
Basin Business Advisor
Rossland / Castlegar region
Book introductory meeting before applying. Advisors review the application informally before it enters the formal queue — this saves months. cbt.org/businesses
City of Rossland
Economic Development Office
rossland.com
Request a letter of support. The City has an interest in retaining remote workers who live locally. Mayor's office may co-sign if the economic development angle is emphasized.
RDKB
Economic Development
Regional District Kootenay Boundary
Inquire about REDI grant eligibility and request a letter of support. RDKB has an economic development mandate that aligns with coworking infrastructure.
Columbia Basin Credit Union
Small Business Lending
Trail / Rossland
Open a relationship as a parallel financing path. Introduce the project before needing a decision. CBCU understands Basin small business context better than national lenders.
Recommended Sequence
1
Week 1-2
Launch demand survey and book CBT Basin Advisor meeting
Start the survey immediately — it takes 2 weeks to collect meaningful data. Book the CBT meeting for Week 3 so you have preliminary results to share. Parallel: register the legal entity.
2
Week 3-4
CBT Basin Advisor meeting — present the business plan
Bring printed copies of Annexes A and B. Focus on the community benefit story: remote work retention, economic resilience, Trail market access. Ask for informal feedback on your application before submission.
3
Week 4-6
Collect letters of support and shortlist spaces
Contact City of Rossland, Rossland Chamber, and RDKB for support letters. Simultaneously visit candidate properties and document lease rates, layout, and landlord appetite for a CBT-contingent clause.
4
Week 6-8
Submit CBT Impact Investment Fund application
Full package: Annexes A, B, C + survey results + support letters + space shortlist + legal entity documents. Also submit or flag the Working Capital Loan if interim financing is needed. CBT review: 60-90 days.
5
Month 3 (CBT Decision)
Go/No-Go: sign lease conditional on CBT approval
If CBT approved: sign the lease and begin fit-out. If CBT delayed: pursue Columbia Basin Credit Union bridge loan. The launch roadmap (Annex C) takes over from this point with a week-by-week pre-launch plan.

On the CBT Community Benefit Test

CBT's Impact Investment Fund is not a grant — it's a low-interest loan that must be repaid, and it requires applicants to demonstrate measurable community or economic benefit to Basin residents. The strongest angle for Rossland Works: enabling remote workers to stay in (or relocate to) Rossland rather than commuting to Trail or leaving the region for larger cities. Frame every conversation with CBT around that story. A coworking space that keeps five families in Rossland is a compelling Basin-benefit case.

Rossland Works
CBT Submission Package — March 2026
Annexes: business-plan.html / financial-model.html / launch-roadmap.html
Prepared with Columbia Basin Trust partnership in mind.
All financial figures net of GST. CBT loan repayment not included in projections.
